content.full_text:
Farn Polrrrcrr Pucrrcrs CouurssroN
P.O. Box 807 . 428 J Street . Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-0886
February 27,2001
Prasanna W. Rasiah, Deputy City Attomey
Office of the City Attorney
1947 Center Street, First Floor
Berkeley, C/.94704
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 4-01-020
Dear Mr. Rasiah:
Re:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of the City of Berkeley's
Community Energy Services Coqporation regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").I
QUESTION
Will the City of Berkeley's Community Energy Services Corporation continue to qualiff
as a "local government agency" subject to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act, after it
ends its affiliation with the City of Berkeley and the Berkeley Energy Commission?
CONCLUSION
Yes. The Community Energy Service Corporation will continue to be a local government
agency, and its members will remain subject to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act,
including the disclosure and disqualification requirements.
' Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-
18996, of the California Code of Regulations.
Ti:+l


File No. A-01-020
Page No. 2
FACTS
On May 21, 1985, the Berkeley City Council authorized the creation of a non-profit
organization to support energy conservation and other similar objectives in Berkeley. The
resolution authorized City Council members to each appoint a citizen to the Energy Services
Organization founding board of directors. On November 20,1986, the board adopted byJaws
and the name Community Energy Services Corporation ("CESC').
The CESC has an annual budget of approximately $650,000, and maintains $361,000 in
contracts with the City for the services it provides (including $290,000 in funding from the
federal Community Development Block Grant program). The balance of roughly $289,000 is
made up of grants from the U.S. Deparfrnent of Energy (to influence local commercial and
residential energy efficiency projects), and fees for services from private sources. Future sources
of revenue have not been set out in detail, but may include the same proportion of City, other
govemmental and private funding.
The Community Energy Services Corporation was formed to promote energy
conservation, renewable energy, local energy self-reliance and environmental quality in the City
of Berkeley. The CESC supplies technical and management services for the City's municipal
energy efficiency projects, commercial energy efficiency services and audits for the City's
Commercial Energy Conservation Ordinance. The non-profit corporation also provides
construction services, energy efficiency education, home repair services and other efforts
focusing on the prudent use of energy and physical resources.
The CESC is currently merged with the Berkeley Energy Commission, such that the
membership of the Energy Commission is the same as that of the CESC Board of Directors. The
Energy Commission advises the City Council on policy matters, such as energy conservation and
altemative energy development. The City Council appoints members to the Energy
Commission, who are automatically co-appointed to the CESC Board of Directors. However,
the CESC plans to end its affiliation with both the City of Berkeley and the Energy Commission.
To approve the separation of the CESC from the Energy Commission, the City Council
plans to pass a resolution, which would also recognizethe CESC as an entity separate from the
City of Berkeley. As part of its separation from the Energy Commission, the articles and by-
laws of the CESC would also be amended to effectuate its status as a separate entity. The CESC
Board would become self-appointing, and the terms of its current members would expire in 18
months or upon the appointrnent of new directors to replace the current Board. Municipal
control over CESC operations would be limited to holding CESC subcontractors to qualification
standards when the corporation bids for construction projects with the City, as with any other
organization that chooses to engage in the competitive bidding process.


File No. A-01-020
Page No. 3
ANALYSN
Section 87300 of the Act requires every state and local agency to adopt a conflict-of-
interest code applicable to its "designated employees." For the purposes of Section 87300,
"agency" is understood to mean any state agency or local govemment agency. (Maas Advice
LJtter, No. A-98-261.) "Local government agency" is defined in Section 82041 as:
"... a county, city or district of any kind including school district,
or any other local or regional political subdivision, or any
department, division, bureau, office, board, commission or other
agency of the foregoing."
Whether a particular entity is a private entity or a local government agency is essentially
a factual determination, which must be made on a case-by-case basis. (In re Yonk (1981) 6
FppC Ops. l.) To that end, the Commission has adopted a four-part factual test for deciding
whether a pariicular entity is a local government agency. (In re Siegel (1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 62.)
Under this"Siegel test," the following four criteria must be considered:
(1) Whether the impetus for formation of the entity originated
with a government agency;
(2) Whether the entity is substantially funded by, or its primary
source of funds is, a government agency;
(3) Whether one of the principal pqposes forwhich the entity
was formed is to provide services or undertake obligations which
public agencies are legally authorized to perform and which, in
fact, they traditionally have performed; and
(a) Whether the entity is treated as a public entity by other
statutory Provisions.
While the Siegeltest is not intended to be a definitive litmus test for determining
whether an entity is a private entity or a local government agency (Vose Advice Letter, No.
I-97-578),through application of its four test criteria, one can draw certain conclusions about
the entity in question. We therefore will apply the Siegel test and from there determine
whether the CESC would still be considered a local govemment agency if it ends its
affiliation with the City of Berkeley and the Energy Commission.
Impetus for Formation
Your letter requesting advice acknowledges that the Berkeley City Council
established the CESC. Because the City is a government agency (Section 82003) and
because the impetus for formation of the CESC originated with a govemment agency, the
first criterion of the Siegel test is met. (Burgess Advice Letter, No. A-00-058; Cool Advice
Letter, No. A-99-084; Francrs Advice Letter, No. A-86-214.)


File No. A-01-020
Page No. 4
Funded by a Government AgencY
Approximately 56Yo of the CESC's budget comes from the City of Berkeley. The
balance of itr fundr come from the federal govemment and private sources. We do not find it
significant that aportion of those funds come from the federal Community Development
Block Grant program. We have taken the position in previous advice letters that if an entity
receives most of its funding from federal grant monies, it meets the second factor. (Matthews
Advice Letter, No. A-99-276; Prestidge Advice Letter, No. A-95-323, modifiedinYose
Advice Letter, No. I-97-578 on issues not affecting the analysis herein.) Although you do
not speciff what percentage of CESC's budget comes from private sources, it is clear that the
CESb is substantially funded by a government agency, and would continue to be so funded
after separating from the City of Berkeley and the Energy Commission. Therefore, the
second criterion of the Siegel test is met.
Services Which Public Agencies Traditionally Perfonn
There have been stafutes addressing energy conservation issues as a governmental
function since at least 1975, when the Warren-Alquist State Energy Resources Conservation
and Development Act, which was passed inl974,became operative. @ub. Res. Code $$
25000 et seq.) You also acknowledge in your letter the 1979 Energy Conservation
Assistance Act (Pub. Res. Code $$ 25410-25421), and the existence of the State Energy
Resources Conservation and Development Commission, which was established by the
Warren-Alquist State Energy Resources Conservation and Development Act. In addition, the
California Altemative Energy and Advanced Transportation Financing Authority Act, which
established the Califomia Alternative Energy and Advanced Transportation Financing
Authority, was enacted in 1980 to, inter alia, "provide industry within this state with an
alternative method of financing in providing and promoting the establishment of . . . (l)
Facilities utilizing altemative methods and sources of energy." (Pub. Res. Code $ 26002)
As the purpose of the CESC is to promote energy conservation, renewable energy,
local energy self-reliance and environmental quality in the City of Berkeley, functions which
are also performed, at least in part, by the organizations listed above, it appears that the
CESC provides services that governmental agencies have traditionally been authorized to
perform, and the third criterion of the Siegel test is met.
Treated as a Public Entity by Other Statutes
You contend in your letter that, after the CESC separates from the City of Berkeley
and the Energy Commission, "municipal control" over the CESC would be the same as for
that of other organizations that bid for construction projects with the City. However,
"municipal control" is not the issue. Rather, it is whether other statutory schemes treat the
CESC as a public agency. Although CESC, as a non-profit public benefit corporation, enjoys


File No. A-01-020
Page No. 5
certain tax benefits also enjoyed by public agencies, you have provided no facts which would
indicate that CESC is treated as a public entity by any other statutes. The ta,x benefits, alone,
are not enough to satisff this criterion of the Siegel test is not met. (Albuquerque Advice
Letter, No. A-88-a22.)
After applyrng the Siegel test to the facts presented in your letter, we find that three of
the four criteria for an entity to be considered a local government agency will still be satisfied
after CESC separates from the City and the Energy Commission. It is not necessary that all
four of the criteria be satisfied for an entity to be considered a local govemment agency.
(O'Shea Advice Letter, No. A-91-570.) It is only necessary that the entity satisfies
enough of the four criteria for its overall character to correspond to that of a local
govemment agency.
Based on the facts you have provided, the functions to be performed by the CESC
after separation will still be governmental in nature and, thus, will continue to present
opportunities for individuals to affect their economic interests through the decisions in which
they participate. One of the purposes of the Act is to assure that public officials perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias. (Section 81001(b).) The Act is to be liberally
construed to accomplish this purpose. (Section 81003.) With this in mind, we believe that
CESC will sufficiently meet the Siegel criteria after separation from the City and the Energy
Commission to still be considered a local govemment agency within the meaning of the Act.
You suggest in your letter that simply forming a new non-profit corporation and
transferring the contract, including those with the City, would eliminate the governmental
impefus for its formation, thus taking the new corporation out of the "local government
agency" sphere. We do not believe that forming a new corporation, alone, would accomplish
this goal. It is only necessary that enough of the Siegel factors be satisfied so that the overall
character of the entity corresponds to that of a local govemment agency. In this case, the
new corporation would still be substantially funded by govemmental agencies, and it would
be pursuing goals established by the City. Therefore, we believe that other factors would
also have to change in order to change the character of the CESC from that of local
government agency to private organization.


5660.
File No. A-01-020
Page No. 6
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322'
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
6
By: Holly
Armstong
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
Ig

Will the City of Berkeley's Community Energy Services Corporation continue to qualiff
as a "local government agency" subject to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act, after it
ends its affiliation with the City of Berkeley and the Berkeley Energy Commission?

Yes. The Community Energy Service Corporation will continue to be a local government
agency, and its members will remain subject to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act,
including the disclosure and disqualification requirements.

QUESTION: Will the City of Berkeley's Community Energy Services Corporation continue to qualiff
as a "local government agency" subject to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act, after it
ends its affiliation with the City of Berkeley and the Berkeley Energy Commission?

CONCLUSION: Yes. The Community Energy Service Corporation will continue to be a local government
agency, and its members will remain subject to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act,
including the disclosure and disqualification requirements.
